coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jing-Jing
Chen, Zhi-Tao
Tang, Duo-Si
Wang, Yao-Bing
Kang, Long-Tian
Yao, Jian-Nian
coredata.dc:description: A simple graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based fluorescence sensing system was developed for rapid, sensitive and low-cost detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in the presence of copper ions. Due to the efficient electron-transfer between GQDs and Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence of GQDs was greatly quenched by Cu2+ ions. Upon addition of ascorbic acid, Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ and the interaction of Cu2+-GQDs was destroyed, which inhibit the quenching and thus leading to fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The detection process is ultrafast and can be completed within 5min. The linear response range of AA was obtained from 0.3μM to 10μM. The detection limit was as low as 94nM, which was comparable to or even better than other reported assays. In addition, this sensing system also showed good selectivity for detecting AA in the presence of different physiological molecules and inorganic ions. Finally, the proposed sensing system was successfully applied to assay AA in real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Copper ions
Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence turn-on
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM (inset) images and (b) size distribution of GQDs. (c) AFM image and (d) the corresponding height profile of GQDs.

The optical properties of GQDs. (a) UV and fluorescence spectra of GQDs in aqueous solution. The inset shows the fluorescence photo of GQDs under UV irradiation. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs containing Cu2+ (100μM) in the prescence of different concentrations of AA. a) 0, b) 0.3μM, c) 0.7μM, d) 1μM, e) 2μM, f) 3μM, g) 5μM, h) 10μM, i) 20μM, j) 50μM, k) 100μM, l) 200μM, m) 500μM. (b) The relationship between the [(F − F 0)/F 0] and the concentration of AA. F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities without and with AA, respectively. The Inset shows a linear relationship in the concentration range from 0.3 to 10μM. The data shown in the figures represent the average of three independent experiments (n =3).

Selectivity of sensing system towards AA and other biologically relevant reactive species. Experimental conditions: AA, urine acid (UA), citric acid (CA), oxalic acid (OA), dopamine (DA), 10μM; mannose (Man), fructose (Fru), glucose (Glc), 100μM; Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, 1000μM.
Schematic illustration of the sensing process for AA based on GQD-Cu2+ probe.
Comparison of the present approach with other reported methods for the detection of AA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of AA in vitamin C tablet (n =5).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS), Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based fluorescence sensing system was developed for rapid, sensitive and low-cost detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in the presence of copper ions. Due to the efficient electron-transfer between GQDs and Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence of GQDs was greatly quenched by Cu2+ ions. Upon addition of ascorbic acid, Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ and the interaction of Cu2+-GQDs was destroyed, which inhibit the quenching and thus leading to fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The detection process is ultrafast and can be completed within 5min. The linear response range of AA was obtained from 0.3μM to 10μM. The detection limit was as low as 94nM, which was comparable to or even better than other reported assays. In addition, this sensing system also showed good selectivity for detecting AA in the presence of different physiological molecules and inorganic ions. Finally, the proposed sensing system was successfully applied to assay AA in real samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA, vitamin C), a water-soluble compound, generally exists in many fruits and plants [1]. It plays an important role in many life processes, such as functioning as an enzyme cofactor, antioxidant, and nutritional factors [2]. As an effective antioxidant, AA is widely used in food, animal feed, beverages, pharmaceutical formulations and cosmetic [3]. It is also can prevent free radical-induced diseases, such as cancer and Parkinson's disease, through the reaction with free radicals [4]. It is well known that the lack of AA will result in scurvy, however excessive intake of AA also easily leads to urinary stone, diarrhea, and stomach convulsion [5]. Therefore, the detection of AA is significant in food industry and diagnostic application.

Up to date, a variety of methods have been explored for AA detection, in which electrochemical method is one of the major approaches [6]. However, the electrochemical oxidation peak potentials for dopamine and uric acid are similar to that for AA, which will seriously interfere in the determination of AA [7]. Other methods, such as chemiluminescence [8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], capillary zone electrophoresis [10], flow-injection spectrophotometry [11] and colorimetric determination [12] have also been reported. Nevertheless, these methods are generally time-consuming, complicated and relatively expensive.

Recently, fluorescent methods have attracted more attention because of their simplicity, convenience and high sensitivity [13]. Fluorescence determination of AA has been achieved with the help of different indicators, such as DNAzyme [14], CdTe quantum dots [15,16], Au nanoclusters [17], Ag nanoparticles [18], LaF3:Ce,Tb nanoparticles [19], and phthalocyanine [20]. It is worthwhile to note that, many of the current fluorescence sensors are “turn-off” model, which might not only cause false-positive results due to other quenchers or environmental stimulus, but also exhibit lower sensitivity than “turn-on” model. Therefore, it's desirable to explore a low-cost and fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for AA with high specificity and sensitivity.

As a new member of the graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which combine the excellent properties of graphene and quantum dots, have attracted considerable attention from scientists in the fields of chemistry, physics, materials, biology and etc [21]. GQDs show considerably low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, excellent solubility, good biocompatibility, and better surface grafting, which make them promising in optoelectronic devices [22–25], sensors [26–34], and bioimaging [35–38]. However, the development and application of GQDs is still in its initial stages, and very little work has been done in the sensing system with “turn-on” model.

In this study, a novel convenient GQDs-based fluorescent turn-on sensor was successfully constructed to detect AA in the presence of Cu2+. The fluorescence of GQDs is first efficiently quenched by Cu2+, and then the fluorescence is restored by the addition of AA. Based on this theory, the proposed sensing system allowed specific and selective detection of AA, and showed lower detection limit than some reported methods. In addition, the successful application of the sensing system in the detection of AA in real sample was also demonstrated.

Graphite powder was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ascorbic acid (AA) and dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine, glucose, fructose and mannose were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co. (USA). Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent co., Ltd. (China). Vitamin C tablets were bought from a local drug store. All reagents were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. Milli-Q ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was used in all experiments.

High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) measurements were performed using a JEOL-2010 (Jeol, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken out using a Nanoscope III a multimode atomic force microscope (Veeco Instruments, USA) in tapping mode to simultaneously collect height and phase data. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a SH-1000 Lab microplate reader (Corona Electric, Japan) at room temperature. Raman spectra were obtained on a Renishaw inVia Raman microspectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were collected by using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan).

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [39,40]. GO (0.2g) was oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (40mL) and HNO3 (120mL) for 12h under ultrasonication (800W, 40kHz). The mixture was then diluted with ultrapure water and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the acids. Then oxidized GO was re-dispersed in ultrapure water and the pH was tuned to 8 with NaOH. After that, the suspension was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated up to 200°C for 12h. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22μm microporous membrane. The collected pale yellow filter solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) overnight and GQDs with strongly fluorescent were obtained.

The sensing solution was prepared by mixing GQDs and aqueous CuSO4 for 5min at room temperature. The final concentration of GQDs and CuSO4 was 4.5μgmL−1 and 100μM, respectively. Then, different concentrations of AA were incubated with the sensing solution for 5min at room temperature. The final volume was 300μL. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the incubated solution were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 310nm.

To test the selectivity, other biologically relevant reactive species with different concentrations were incubated with the sensing solution for 5min at room temperature. The rest of the procedure was the same as described above.

A piece of vitamin C tablet was ground and dissolved in ultrapure water. The mixture was sonicated for 10min for fully dissolution. The solution was further diluted with ultrapure water so that the AA concentration in the final solution could meet the linear range of the standard curves. Following that, the diluted solution was spiked with different concentrations of AA. The spiked samples were then incubated with the sensing solution for 5min at room temperature. The rest of the procedure was the same as described above.

GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. The as-prepared GQDs had a narrow size distribution largely in the range of 2.5–4.5nm with an average diameter of 3.5nm (Fig. 1a and b). The high resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a, inset) clearly shows the lattice spacing of 0.21nm, corresponding to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene ((100) facet). Fig. 1c and d shows the AFM analysis of GQDs. The height profiles reveal that the typical topographic height of the GQDs were in the range of 0.7–1.4nm.

The GQDs suspension shows a typically absorption peak at ca. 238nm which was attributed to the π–π* transitions of CC bonds (Fig. 2a). An absorption peak at ca. 309nm was also observed. Subsequent fluorescence characterization indicated that the GQDs can emit strong blue fluorescence under excitation at 365nm with a UV lamp (Fig. 2a, inset). As shown in Fig. 2b, GQDs also exhibits an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. With increasing excitation wavelengths, the emission peak position shifts to longer wavelengths and the intensity decreases rapidly. The maximum emission intensity from GQDs was achieved at 425nm when they excited at 310nm.

The strong fluorescence of the GQDs was found quenched in the present of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. S2, when the concentration of Cu2+ ions accumulated 100μM, up to 80% quenching of the fluorescence emission was observed.

It is well known that AA is a strong reducing agent, and it could reduce copper from cupric (Cu2+) to the cuprous (Cu+). Therefore, the quenched fluorescence of the GQDs by Cu2+ can be recovered upon the addition of AA. Having the above in mind, a rapid, sensitive sensing method based on the fluorescence change of GQDs was developed for the detection of AA. Scheme 1 illustrates the “off-to-on” mechanism of GQDs for assay AA.

Fig. S3 confirms the feasibility of the presented GQDs-based sensing assay. GQDs possessed bright blue emission fluorescence (Fig. S3, curve a), and the fluorescence of GQDs was scarcely influenced by the addition of only AA (Fig. S3 curve b). However, in the presence of 100μM Cu2+, up to 80% quenching of the fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. S3, curve c). Subsequently, the GQDs had a significant fluorescence enhancement upon addition of AA, and the emission peak position shows a red shift from 425nm to 440nm (Fig. S3, curve d).

Besides Cu2+, the effect of 9 other kinds of metal ions, including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ on the fluorescence response of GQDs were investigated. The result in Fig. S4 shows that the fluorescence of GQDs did not significantly change in the presence of other metal ions except Cu2+ and Fe3+. Although Fe3+ can also quench the fluorescence of GQDs, its interference can be easily avoided by using sodium hexametaphosphate (SHPP) as a chelating agent [41]. As shown in Fig. S5, the introduction of Fe3+ into GQDs-Cu2+ mixtures had no influence on the detection of Cu2+ in the presence of SHPP. Therefore Fe3+ can be successfully masked in this system with little effect on the detection of Cu2+.

The kinetic behavior of the present AA detection system was studied by monitoring the fluorescence intensity as a function of time, as shown in Fig. S6. Curve I shows the time-dependent fluorescence quenching of GQDs in the presence of Cu2+. The quench process was a fairly rapid process. It reached equilibrium in 1min. Curve II shows the fluorescence restoration of GQDs-Cu2+ in the presence of AA as a function of incubation time. The reduced of the Cu2+ to Cu+ was relatively slow and needed almost 5min to reach the equilibrium.

It is well known that many divalent metal ions are fluorescence quenchers and work via ground-state complexation, charge transfer phenomena, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, etc. [42]. Among them, Cu2+ is paramagnetic ion with an unfilled d shell and quenches the fluorescence via electron or energy transfer [43]. In this system, the mechanism of energy transfer can be ruled out due to the absence of overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs and the absorption spectrum of Cu2+ (Fig. S7). Hence, the quenching effect presumably results from the effective electron transfer that occurred between Cu2+ and GQDs. To further study the quenching process, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of GQDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were studied. Decay profiles are shown in Fig. S8. GQDs alone have a lifetime of about 5.05ns, while a decrease of lifetime of 3.79ns was observed in the presence of Cu2+. This result reveals that the quenching mechanism of GQDs by Cu2+ was dynamic quenching mechanism.

To ensure the presented sensing system can be used for sensitive quantification of AA, the fluorescence responses induced by AA at different concentrations were evaluated. Fig. 3a shows the fluorescence-emission spectra of the system upon the addition of different concentrations of AA. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence enhancement factors [(F − F 0)/F 0] induced by AA increased upon increasing the concentration of AA up to 500μM. The inset of Fig. 3b clearly represents the linear relationship between fluorescence signal and the concentration of AA in the range from 0.3 to 10μM. The regression equation is Y =0.1722X +0.1456, where Y and X denote the fluorescence peak intensity and target concentration, respectively. The corresponding correlation coefficient of calibration curve is 0.9985. And the sensing system can detect AA in concentrations as low as 94nM. Table 1 lists the comparison of the proposed sensing system and other approaches for the detection of AA. Our present work provides a limit of detection that is comparable to those previously reported sensor based on colorimetry [44], electrochemistry [45], chemiluminescence [46] and fluorescence techniques [15,17].

In order to evaluate the detection specificity of the present detection system, the degree of other biologically relevant reactive species, such as metal ions and reduing agents was evaluated under the same experimental conditions as those involved in the AA assay. As shown in Fig. 4 , the sensing system exhibits a slight fluorescence response to other biologically relevant reactive species. However, there was significant fluorescence enhancement occurs after the introduction of AA. These results clearly demonstrate that this sensing system also shows high selectivity toward AA. Thus, the proposed sensing system could be used as a sensitive and selective platform for AA detection. Because histidine can also combine with Cu2+, fluorescence recovery was also observed in the presence of histidine (Fig. S9). Therefore, histidine must be removed or masked in some practical applications.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the practical application of this sensing system, we applied a standard addition method to estimate the concentrations of AA in vitamin C table. A series of AA at concentrations of 2, 4 and 5μM were spiked into vitamin C tablet solution. These samples were then incubated with GQDs-Cu2+ before the fluorescence spectrum were measured. The analytical results for the biological fluids are shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of spiked ascorbic acid in the diluted biological fluids ranged from 97% to 99%, thus demonstrating the potential applicability of the GQDs-based “turn-on” fluorescent probe for the quantitative detection of ascorbic acid in real sample.

A simple fluorescent probe has been developed to detect AA based on “off-to-on” mechanism of GQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence “turn-on” sensing system enabled quick detection of AA with high sensitivity and selectivity. The linear response range between 0.3 and 10μM and the limit of detection 94nM for AA was achieved. Furthermore, it was successful to detect AA in real samples with satisfactory results. In conclusion, the present work provided a simple, rapid, low-cost and highly efficient GQDs-based fluorescence “turn-on” approach for the detection of AA, which can be used as a promising candidate for the determination of AA in food industry and diagnostic application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21252001), the Special Project of National Major Scientific Equipment Development of China (No. 2012YQ120060).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.02.019.

Luminescent graphene quantum dots as new fluorescent materials for environmental and biological applications
Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices
Graphene quantum dots–carbon nanotube hybrid arrays for supercapacitors

Efficiency enhancement of perovskite solar cells through fast electron extraction: the role of graphene quantum dots

Using graphene quantum dots as photoluminescent probes for protein kinase sensing
Boronic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive glucose determination in microdialysate
A universal immunosensing strategy based on regulation of the interaction between graphene and graphene quantum dots
Self-assembled graphene quantum dots induced by cytochrome c: A novel biosensor for trypsin with remarkable fluorescence enhancement

Graphene quantum dots as fluorescence probes for turn-off sensing of melamine in the presence of Hg2+
Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
Graphene quantum dots/gold electrode and its application in living cell H2O2 detection
Surface coating of graphene quantum dots using mussel-inspired polydopamine for biomedical optical imaging
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
Flexible graphene films via the filtration of water-soluble noncovalent functionalized graphene sheets
Ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet: A highly efficient fluorosensor for rapid, ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+
A DNA-scaffolded silver nanoclusters/Cu2+ ensemble as a turn-on fluorescent probe for histidine
Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets: one-step, high-yield synthesis and application for Cu2+ detection

Flow injection system with chemiluminometric detection for enzymatic determination of ascorbic acid

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingjing Liu received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Fuzhou University in 2013. She is a research assistant at Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences (FJIRSM). Her research interests focus on the biochemical analysis applications of advanced nanomaterials.

Zhitao Chen obtained his M.S. degree from Fuzhou University. In 2008, he joined the Fuqing Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau. He is now studying for Ph.D. degree at Fuzhou University. His current research interests are the synthesis and pretreatment application of advanced nanomaterials.

Duosi Tang obtained his B.S. degree from China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He is studying for M.S. degree at FJIRSM and Fuzhou University. His current research interests are the synthesis and properties of graphene quantum dots.

Yaobing Wang received his Ph.D. degree at Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ICCAS) in 2008. He has been a professor since 2012 in FJIRSM. His research interest focuses on the applications of advanced carbon materials in energy storage, super-capacitor, lithium battery and fuel cell.

Longtian Kang received his Ph.D. degree at ICCAS in 2008. From 2008 to 2010, he was a postdoctoral research associate at the University of Groningen and University of Trieste. In 2012, he joined in FJIRSM as a professor. Now his research focuses on the chemistry, optical spectroscopy and bioapplications of graphene-based composite materials.

Jiannian Yao received his Ph.D. degree with Prof. Akira Fujishima at Tokyo University in 1993. He joined the Institute of Photographic Chemistry, CAS, in 1995, and now he is a Professor of Chemistry in ICCAS. He is currently serving as a permanent member and chairman of the Chinese Chemical Society (CCS), and is the vice president of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC). His research interests are focused on organic nanomaterials and inorganic optofunctional materials with photochromic and photocatalytic properties.
